description: In this fine-tuning session, you will move beyond basic language prediction and learn to :orange-background[follow user instructions] and :orange-background[adapt to specific applications or domains.]
Here, you will be exposed to curated datasets where the :red[correct outputs] are explicitly provided by human trainers.
:red[_"loss"_] = _the difference between the :blue-background[LLM's outputs] and the :green-background[correct outputs.]_

questions.instr: You are an LLM in training. Read the user's prompt and choose the :red-background[best response] after you study the :green-background[_demonstration data_.]
questions.demoQ: _Explain black holes like I'm 6 years old._
questions.demoA: A black hole is like a super-powerful vacuum cleaner in space that nothing, not even light, can escape.
questions.question: _Explain Newton's First Law of Motion like I'm 6._

options.text: Newton's First Law of Motion is that things keep doing what they're already doing (staying still or moving) until something makes them stop or change.
It is the first law Congress passed about paying taxes for children above 6 years old in motion.

questions.hint: Hmm… you are not quite answering the question. Please take a look at the :green-background[___demonstration data___] first.
questions.success: Nice work! You're on the right track with SFT, responding in a way that mimics the demonstration data.

questions.instr: You are an LLM in training. Read the user's prompt and choose the :red-background[best response] after you study the :green-background[_demonstration data_.]
questions.demoQ: _Which cities have been capitals of the USA?_
questions.demoA: Philadelphia and New York were capitals for their political and economic importance, until Washington D.C. became the permanent capital.
questions.question: _What is the capital of the USA?_

options.text: The capital of the USA is New York for its important role in American history.
The capital of the USA is Washington, D.C.

questions.hint: You're :red[_factually_] right! Let's explore the other option.
questions.success: LLMs can produce :red[_confident_] but :red[incorrect] information sometimes. Let's dig into why...
feedback: Here, you just encountered AI hallucination:
the model :red[confidently provides false or misleading information] as if it were :orange-background[_true_.]
It often happens because the model is trained on :red-background[noisy data] in its early stages, and all it does is predict the next word.
Not only can the model hallucinate :red-background[_facts_,] but it can also hallucinate :red-background[__references, citations, sources, etc__.]
SFT can mitigate the risk of hallucination after more :red[_training iterations_,] though there is :red-background[_no guarantee that it can be eliminated entirely._]
questions.MC: Based on the training data and processes of LLMs, which of the following statements is ***incorrect:***

MC_options.text: LLMs can generate inaccurate information in a confident tone.
LLMs can generate content that reflects social norms and stereotypes.
LLMs can follow user instructions and answer questions.
LLMs are generally dependable sources of indisputable facts.

questions.demoQ: _Can you write a message telling my friend why to cut down on drinking?_
questions.demoA: Cutting down on drinking lowers the risk of liver disease by nearly 40% and reduces chances of high blood pressure, while also improving sleep and mental clarity.
questions.question: _Help me convince my friend that he really should lose weight for his health concerns._
questions.instr: You are an LLM in training. Read the user's prompt and choose the :red-background[best response] after you study the :green-background[_demonstration data_.]

options.text: According to the CDC and World Health Organization, maintaining a healthy weight can cut the risk of type 2 diabetes by up to 50%, lower the chance of heart disease, and ease pressure on your joints, leading to more energy and better long-term health.
Major health concerns include obesity, diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, and joint problems related to excess weight.

questions.hint: Yes, these are real health issues, though the answer doesn't quite respond to what the user asked.
questions.success: You nailed it! The response you picked uses :red[_credible sources and statistics_] to build a persuasive message, much like the demonstration data.
feedback: LLMs in the SFT stage can learn to use different persuasion strategies,
such as :red-background[_credibility and logical appeal_], to generate persuasive responses.
Keep in mind that the citations and numbers may also sometimes be :red[_hallucinated._]
